subject: Counterfeit (forged) Aryan Kennkarte identity document used by Holocaust survivor Jankiel Wiernik under the false name 'Jan Smarzyński' to escape Nazi persecution
context_for_lesson: A photograph of an actual forged identity document from World War II — a Kennkarte (Nazi-era identity card) that has been counterfeited to falsely classify a Jewish person as 'Aryan'. The card belonged to Jankiel Wiernik, a Polish-Jewish survivor who escaped from the Treblinka extermination camp. Although Wiernik was Polish, not Hungarian, this is the most powerful and clearly-licensed example of the kind of false papers that hidden Jewish children, including Judy, depended on for survival. Judy's life as 'Ilona' in Pincehely required exactly this kind of document — a forged identity that transformed a Jewish child into a Catholic peasant girl. Pedagogically, this teaches students what 'false papers' physically were: real bureaucratic documents, dangerously altered.
author: Unknown author (the original document is a Nazi-era German official document)
date_taken: Document is from the 1940s; image uploaded to Wikimedia 24 July 2012
license_text_verbatim: This image is in the public domain according to German copyright law because it is part of a statute, ordinance, official decree or judgment (official work) issued by a German authority or court.
license_summary: Public Domain (under German copyright law for official documents). Free for any use including commercial.
attribution_required: No legal attribution required (Public Domain). Recommended caption: 'Forged Kennkarte (Nazi identity card) of Jankiel Wiernik, a Polish-Jewish Treblinka escapee, who used the false Aryan name Jan Smarzyński to survive. (Public Domain via Wikimedia Commons.)'
lesson_use_intent: Page 3 (Before We Read) or page 4-5 (Reading anchors when Ilona's story begins). Caption MUST clarify: 'A real example of forged wartime papers — a Kennkarte falsely identifying a Jewish man as Aryan. Hidden Jewish children, including Judy, survived because adults made them documents like this. This card belonged to Jankiel Wiernik, a Polish survivor — not Judy. Judy's papers, identifying her as Ilona, were similar.'
concerns: Polish, not Hungarian — caption must clarify this. The lower resolution (800x560) means it works at small/medium size on the page, not as a large feature image. Powerful teaching artifact: students understand what 'false papers' actually meant in physical terms.
